stem: Evaluate \( \arcsin\!\left(\sin\dfrac{[[k]]\pi}{[[n]]}\right) \). Give your answer as a value in \( \left[-\tfrac{\pi}{2},\tfrac{\pi}{2}\right] \).
solution: \( \arcsin \) only undoes \( \sin \) directly when the angle already lies in \( \left[-\tfrac{\pi}{2},\tfrac{\pi}{2}\right] \); otherwise it returns the angle in that range with the same sine. Reducing \( \dfrac{[[k]]\pi}{[[n]]} \) to its equivalent principal-branch angle gives \( [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This reports the original angle without checking whether it lies in the principal branch \( [-\pi/2,\pi/2] \).
This uses the reference-angle rule for cosine/the second quadrant instead of the correct sine branch reduction.
This flips the sign of the original angle instead of reducing it into the principal branch.
This reduces the angle mod \( n \) directly on the fraction \( k/n \) instead of finding the equivalent principal-branch angle.
This adds a full extra \( \pi \) instead of reflecting into the principal branch.
This subtracts an extra \( \pi \) instead of reflecting into the principal branch.

stem: Evaluate \( \arccos\!\left(\cos\dfrac{[[k]]\pi}{[[n]]}\right) \). Give your answer as a value in \( [0,\pi] \).
solution: \( \arccos \) only undoes \( \cos \) directly when the angle already lies in \( [0,\pi] \); otherwise it returns the angle in that range with the same cosine. Reducing \( \dfrac{[[k]]\pi}{[[n]]} \) to its equivalent angle in \( [0,\pi] \) gives \( [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This reports the original angle without checking whether it lies in \( [0,\pi] \).
This flips the sign of the original angle instead of reducing it into \( [0,\pi] \).
This reflects the angle across \( 2\pi \) rather than finding the equivalent angle in \( [0,\pi] \) with the same cosine.
This takes the absolute value of the reduced fraction \( k/n \) directly rather than finding the true equivalent angle.
This adds a full extra \( \pi \) instead of correctly reflecting into \( [0,\pi] \).
This subtracts an extra \( \pi \) instead of correctly reflecting into \( [0,\pi] \).

stem: Evaluate \( \arctan\!\left(\tan\dfrac{[[k]]\pi}{[[n]]}\right) \). Give your answer as a value in \( \left(-\tfrac{\pi}{2},\tfrac{\pi}{2}\right) \).
solution: \( \arctan \) only undoes \( \tan \) directly when the angle already lies in \( \left(-\tfrac{\pi}{2},\tfrac{\pi}{2}\right) \); since \( \tan \) has period \( \pi \), \( \arctan(\tan\theta) \) equals \( \theta \) shifted by a multiple of \( \pi \) into that interval. This gives \( [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This reports the original angle without shifting it into \( (-\pi/2,\pi/2) \).
This flips the sign of the original angle instead of shifting it by a multiple of \( \pi \).
This uses the cosine branch's reflection rule instead of tangent's \( \pi \)-periodic shift.
This adds a full extra \( \pi \) instead of the correct shift.
This subtracts an extra \( \pi \) instead of the correct shift.
This reduces the fraction \( k/n \) mod \( n \) directly instead of finding the true equivalent angle.

solution: From the table of exact values, \( \arcsin\dfrac{[[p]]}{2} \) and \( \arccos\dfrac{[[q]]}{2} \) are each a special angle; adding them gives \( [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This subtracts the second term instead of adding it.
This swaps which special-value table (\( \arcsin \) vs \( \arccos \)) applies to which argument.
This adds the raw input ratios instead of the angles they represent.
This multiplies the two angles instead of adding them.
This adds a stray \( \pi \) to the correct sum.
This subtracts a stray \( \pi \) from the correct sum.
